coaching.coaching: Pin down executable check for broad per-file ignores with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: executable check for broad per-file ignores

coaching.coaching: Plan how deterministic verification steps for all acceptance criteria will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: deterministic verification steps for all acceptance criteria

coaching.coaching: Pin down automated check for S-family rule absence with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: automated check for S-family rule absence

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Augment AC3 with a concrete proving command that asserts no per-file `noqa` overrides were added, e.g. `grep -rn 'per-file-ignores.*RUF' pyproject.toml` returning zero matches, or add a ratchet-test assertion.
evidence: 'configuration inspection' is open-ended — no grep pattern, no file path, no pass/fail criterion is stated
The plan rejects 'broad per-file ignores' but provides no command that would detect them if added
findings.finding: AC3's 'configuration inspection' is not a deterministic, executable check; it does not specify what command or artifact an agent should inspect to confirm no broad per-file ignores were added.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'verified by make lint and configuration inspection.'
scenarios: An agent runs `make lint` (green), judges 'configuration inspection' satisfied by a visual scan, but a per-file ignore was added to pyproject.toml and goes undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'and `grep -E "per-file-ignores" pyproject.toml | grep -c "RUF"` returns 0'.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'verified by the diff' in AC4 with a deterministic proving command, e.g. `git diff HEAD pyproject.toml | grep -E '^\+.*"S[0-9]' | wc -l` asserting zero, or a dedicated ratchet test assertion that the selected rules list contains no S-family entries.
evidence: 'verified by the diff' — no concrete shell command is given; a diff can be incomplete or misread without an anchored grep/assertion
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (4): fixture invalidity / absence of a deterministic proving command
findings.finding: AC4's proving method ('verified by the diff') is a manual review artifact, not a deterministic in-session command; an automated check that S-family rules are absent from pyproject.toml changes would be more reliable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'Ruff S rules and Semgrep remain unchanged; verified by the diff.'
scenarios: An executing agent completes the AC by eyeballing a git diff and signs off, but a stray S-rule addition slipped in — the AC passes without catching the regression.
CI has no command to assert S-rule stability, so the criterion cannot be automatically re-evaluated in the closing PR.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'verified by `git diff HEAD pyproject.toml` showing no S-rule additions AND by `python -c "import tomllib; cfg=tomllib.load(open(\"pyproject.toml\",\"rb\")); assert not any(r.startswith(\"S\") for r in cfg[\"tool\"][\"ruff\"][\"lint\"][\"select\"])"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that explicitly requires the full default test suite (and any focused regression tests for nontrivial manual edits) to pass, matching the 'Regression' step already described in the Testing section.
evidence: Testing section states: 'Regression: focused tests for nontrivial manual edits and the full default suite pass.'
ABSENCE: None of AC1–AC4 reference the full default test suite or focused regression tests for manual edits; AC3 only covers 'make lint and configuration inspection'.
findings.finding: The Testing section describes a 'Regression' step ('focused tests for nontrivial manual edits and the full default suite pass') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this regression gate, leaving described work uncovered by a criterion.
findings.location: Testing section ('Regression: focused tests for nontrivial manual edits and the full default suite pass') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC4)
scenarios: An implementer resolves all RUF findings and passes all four ACs but silently breaks existing tests via a manual code edit; the plan provides no AC to catch this regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] The full default test suite passes with no new failures after all manual edits; verified by running the project's standard test command (e.g., make test or pytest).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief AGENTS.md or inline design note explaining the config-contract test pattern (what invariants `test_ruff_ratchet.py` enforces, why it lives in unit tests rather than CI scripts, and how to update it when the deferred-rules list evolves).
evidence: 'add a config contract test that requires RUF selection and explicit deferral of RUF001-003' — a novel pattern with no doc update mentioned
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
Absence of any guidance for future contributors on how/when to modify the ratchet test when deferral scope changes
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pattern — a unit test (`tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py`) that asserts the shape of pyproject.toml linting configuration — but includes no documentation update (ADR, AGENTS.md, or design note) explaining why this meta-testing contract exists, what invariants it enforces, and how to extend it when the deferred-rules list changes.
scenarios: A new contributor adds a rule to the deferred list without knowing the ratchet test must be updated, causing a confusing test failure
The pattern is extended to other rule families without a documented convention, leading to inconsistent contract tests across the project
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to AGENTS.md under a 'Linting contract tests' heading that explains: (1) `test_ruff_ratchet.py` asserts that pyproject.toml selects RUF and explicitly defers only the approved set; (2) any change to the deferred list requires updating both pyproject.toml and the ratchet test; (3) this pattern should be followed for future rule-family rollouts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the two AC1-required assertions (RUF in `select`; RUF001-003 in `ignore`) should be added as new test functions in the existing `tests/unit/test_error_handling_convention.py` (which already has the tomllib + subprocess ruff machinery) rather than creating a new `test_ruff_ratchet.py` that rebuilds that infrastructure.
evidence: test_error_handling_convention.py already imports tomllib, reads data['tool']['ruff']['lint']['per-file-ignores'] from pyproject.toml, and runs subprocess ruff check with --select flags and --stdin-filename — the identical machinery the proposed file would need.
test_mypy_ratchet.py already reads data['tool']['mypy']['overrides'] via tomllib and asserts a config key baseline — the same pattern needed to assert RUF is in select and RUF001-003 are in ignore.
Two new test functions (e.g. test_ruf_is_selected, test_ruf001_003_are_deferred) added to test_error_handling_convention.py would fully satisfy AC1 without a new file.
ABSENCE: the plan does not justify why a dedicated file is preferred over extending the existing convention test.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a net-new `tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py` whose implementation machinery (tomllib pyproject.toml reads + subprocess ruff check invocations with specific --select flags) is already present in `tests/unit/test_error_handling_convention.py`. Creating a second file rebuilds rather than extends that existing infrastructure.
scenarios: A maintainer later adding a third linting ratchet test encounters split machinery between two files instead of one consolidated convention test.
The new file duplicates the tomllib + subprocess ruff boilerplate, increasing maintenance surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_ruf_family_is_selected()` and `test_ruf001_003_are_explicitly_deferred()` as new functions in `tests/unit/test_error_handling_convention.py`, reusing the existing `_REPO_ROOT`, `tomllib.loads(...)`, and `subprocess.run([..., 'ruff', 'check', ...])` scaffolding already in that file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a traceable reference (ticket ID, ADR link, or inline rationale) for 'the approved RUF rollout' so the RUF001/002/003 deferral decision can be audited independently of this plan.
evidence: Search for 'approved RUF rollout' returns zero hits in repo source/docs/config files.
Search for 'RUF001', 'RUF002', 'RUF003' returns zero hits in any project file (only in plan-review cache).
pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E", "F", "W", "I", "UP", "B", "BLE001", "T201", "SIM115"] — no RUF family present, consistent with never having been approved or attempted.
No ticket ID, ADR, or runbook entry for 'RUF rollout' found in docs/ or .rebar/.
findings.finding: The plan's Context section references 'The approved RUF rollout' as a prior decision that authorizes and scopes this work, but no approval record, ticket reference, design doc, or configuration entry for this approval is locatable anywhere in the repository. Searching for 'approved RUF rollout', 'RUF001', 'RUF002', and 'RUF003' across all repo files returns zero hits outside cached plan-review artifacts.
findings.location: Context / Problem section: 'The approved RUF rollout must clear existing RUF100 stale suppressions…'
scenarios: If the 'approval' is actually a verbal/external decision and no codebase artifact records it, the plan's constraint ('explicit deferral of RUF001-003 from this approved scope') has no auditable basis — a future reviewer cannot confirm the deferral was deliberate rather than accidental.
If the approval document exists elsewhere (e.g. a linked ticket), the plan should cite it so implementers can confirm the exact scope of what was approved before touching pyproject.toml.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context section, replace 'The approved RUF rollout' with a cited reference, e.g. 'The RUF rollout approved in [ticket-id / ADR-NNN]' or add an inline rationale explaining why RUF001-003 are deferred (e.g. 'RUF001/002/003 flag intentional Unicode in prose/UI strings — deferred by design').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Approach section explaining *why* fix-before-select ordering was chosen (e.g., selecting RUF first and suppressing inline would introduce new noqa debt and trigger the RUF100-under-narrow-select artifact; fixing first keeps the tree clean at every step).
evidence: Plan Approach: 'First add a config contract test … Use the pinned Ruff fixer only for safe mechanical edits, review every diff, then manually resolve remaining RUF findings across src and tests. Remove stale noqa comments reported by RUF100 only after confirming the underlying currently-selected rule remains clean.'

No sentence in the Approach section explains why fix-first ordering was selected (e.g., 'fix before selecting avoids introducing new noqa suppression debt and matches the RUF100-under-narrow-select artifact documented in the maintenance runbook').
ABSENCE: no positive rationale sentence connecting the ordering choice to its benefits.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'fix-before-select' ordering and the incremental fixer-then-manual-resolve approach but never states a positive rationale for *why* this ordering was chosen over alternatives (e.g., select-first with inline suppression followed by incremental removal). Per G6 sub-item 4, the absence of a positive rationale for the chosen approach is a finding.
scenarios: An implementer might deviate from the ordering (e.g., add RUF to select immediately and suppress inline first) if the rationale is unclear, reintroducing the noqa debt the Rejected section explicitly guards against.
A reviewer evaluating the plan cannot verify the approach is defensible without a stated rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Approach section: 'This fix-before-select ordering is chosen because adding RUF to `select` before resolving findings would require inline `# noqa: RUFXXX` suppressions that immediately become RUF100 stale-suppression candidates once fixed — exactly the noqa debt the plan aims to eliminate. It also avoids the RUF100-under-narrow-select artifact documented in `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
